nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs at approximately 2-10 m depth, living in close association with the host anemones Entacmaea quadricolor and Heteractis crispa.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Central Pacific reef; coral reef areas with hard substrate hosting bubble-tip and leathery sea anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open mid-water swimming space to establish territory
host anemone optional (Entacmaea quadricolor bubble-tip anemone preferred, Heteractis crispa also accepted)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, marine pellets, flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended. In the wild has been documented eating zooplankton, small crustaceans, algae and polychaete worms.

goodWith: peaceful and semi-aggressive reef fish of similar or smaller size
reef-safe invertebrates including shrimps and snails
avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
small timid species that can be bullied at feeding
large predatory fish that may eat smaller clownfish

summary: The Barberi Clownfish is a deep reddish-orange species from Fiji, Tonga, and American Samoa, belonging to the Tomato Complex group of clownfish. It is distinguished by a single bright white bar behind the head and is generally considered hardy and reef-safe. Wild individuals are commonly hosted by bubble-tip and leathery sea anemones.
careNotes: The Barberi Clownfish (Amphiprion barberi) was formally described by Allen, Drew, and Kaufman in 2008. Before that, populations from Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa had often been sold as the Australian Clownfish (A. rubrocinctus) or mistaken for a color variant of the Cinnamon Clownfish (A. melanopus). As a member of the Tomato Complex, it tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but can be aggressively territorial toward other clownfish or similarly shaped damselfish. It is best kept singly or as a single bonded pair, and should not be mixed with other clownfish species. Like all clownfish, the Barberi Clownfish is a protandrous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals develop first as males and can change to female when a social situation calls for it. In a bonded pair, the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male. Host anemones are not required in captivity, but this species readily associates with the bubble-tip anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) and the leathery sea anemone (Heteractis crispa) when they are available. Captive-bred specimens are now available from specialist marine aquaculture producers and are generally easier to acclimate than wild-caught fish, accepting prepared foods readily.
breedingNotes: The Barberi Clownfish has been bred in captivity, with commercial captive breeding established by 2012. It is generally considered moderate difficulty for an experienced aquarist, comparable to other members of the Tomato Complex. Like other clownfish, it is an egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs (laid on a surface rather than scattered in open water) on a cleaned flat area, typically near the host anemone. The male guards and aerates the clutch. Nest sizes of 500 or more eggs have been reported. Eggs hatch in roughly 8 to 11 days depending on water temperature, and larvae require rotifers followed by enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page (Captive-Bred Barberi Clownfish). The listed root URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/clownfish did not surface this species on the catalog page, but the canonical product page was located via site search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Allen, Drew & Kaufman, 2008), family (Pomacentridae), max size (8.6 cm SL), preferred temperature range (25.8-29°C mean 27°C), Central Pacific distribution (Fiji, Tonga, American Samoa), depth range (2-10 m), and host-anemone associations (Entacmaea quadricolor, Heteractis crispa).

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of Tomato Complex placement, white-head-bar coloration that distinguishes A. barberi from A. melanopus (Cinnamon) and A. frenatus (Tomato), and historical taxonomic confusion with A. rubrocinctus. Notes nest sizes of 500+ eggs are common.

additional.name: Sea & Reef Aquaculture
additional.notes: Fourth-source confirmation of bold/territorial temperament toward conspecifics and clownfish in general, peaceful behaviour toward other species, host anemones (bubble-tip and magnificent), and omnivorous diet. Reports a larger maximum size (11.5 cm / 4.5 in) than FishBase or LiveAquaria; used in the adult-size cross-source average.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary in source-urls.json for fish-saltwater slice. Per-species wiki page not separately verified; Sea & Reef and ORA were substituted as additional cross-verification sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species is established at commercial scale. As a demersal egg-layer, the fish deposits its eggs on a flat cleaned surface, with clutch sizes of 500 or more commonly reported. Larvae require rotifer cultures in the first days after hatching, transitioning to enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
